M06-1-title.title: M06 Comprehensive Practice & Real-World Application
M06-1-title.subtitle: Analyze, Design, and Discuss Content Using Framework Language
M06-1-title.notes: The capstone comprehensive practice session. Core task: analyze videos using BMS framework + group design content plans.

M06-1-review.title: Bone-Meat-Soul Three-Layer Architecture Review
M06-1-review.studentContent:
🦴 Bone Layer

Defines Video Structure

  • Section — Building blocks, each with a specific psychological function
  • Skeleton — Structural framework, how Sections are combined
  • Strategy — Persuasion techniques, L0/L1/L2 three-tier system

👻 Soul Layer

Explains Why It Works

  • Target Audience target_audience
  • Trigger Scenario trigger_scenario
  • Conflict Type conflict_type
  • Reward Type reward_type
  • Emotion Arc emotion_arc

🥩 Meat Layer

Defines How It's Presented

  • Visual Style visual_style
  • Pacing Control pacing
  • Voice Design voice_type
  • Visual Elements text_overlay
All three layers work together — none can be missing.
M06-1-review.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Welcome back. Next up is the most important hands-on session of the two-day training — the comprehensive practice.

We've finished learning all three layers of Bone-Meat-Soul. Now it's time to test what you've learned — we're going to do the reverse: break down existing videos and identify their Bone, Meat, and Soul.

Let's start with a quick review of the three-layer architecture.
M06-1-review.notes: Quick review of the three layers to activate memory — not a re-lecture. Don't drag on too long.

M06-1-goal.title: Core Objective of This Module
M06-1-goal.studentContent:
❌ Previous Discussion Style
  • "This one's pretty good"
  • "This doesn't work"
  • "It's not engaging enough"
✅ Framework Language Discussion
  • "The Skeleton is HPVC, HOOK section uses SUSPENSE strategy"
  • "The Soul layer's conflict_type isn't clear enough"
  • "Meat's pacing should be adjusted to high"
Analyze content, design content, and discuss content using framework language
Not just filling out forms, but truly internalizing this way of thinking
M06-1-goal.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Before, when you saw a video, you might just say "this is good" or "this doesn't work." But now it's different — you should be able to say "Its Skeleton is HPVC, the HOOK section uses SUSPENSE strategy, but the Soul layer's conflict_type isn't clear enough."

That's discussing problems using framework language. In this module, our core objective is — Analyze content, design content, and discuss content using framework language.
M06-1-goal.notes: Emphasize that 'discussing with framework language' is the core goal of this module — not just filling out forms.

M06-2-title.title: Part 2
M06-2-title.subtitle: Video Playback + Independent Analysis
M06-2-title.notes: Play pre-selected practice videos; participants independently fill out analysis forms.

M06-2-rules.title: Analysis Rules
M06-2-rules.studentContent:
Video Analysis Exercise

📋 Analysis Steps

  1. First watch: Just watch, feel the overall impression
  2. Second watch: Watch and fill out the form, using the "BMS Complete Analysis Form"

🎯 Identify the Most Obvious Features First

  • What Skeleton does this video use?
  • What type of Strategy does the HOOK section use?
  • Who is the Soul layer's target audience?
⚠️ You don't need to fill in every field. Write down uncertain parts first — you can align during the group discussion later.
M06-2-rules.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
OK, now let's do the first exercise — video analysis.

I'll play a video, and each of you will analyze it using the "BMS Complete Analysis Form." First watch: just watch and feel the overall impression. Second watch: watch and fill out the form.

You don't need to fill in every field — identify the most obvious features first. You have 5 minutes to complete the analysis independently.
M06-2-rules.notes: Distribute the 'BMS Complete Analysis Form.' Video selection: moderately complex company video, recommended Tier A Skeleton (e.g., HPVC or HBVC).

M06-2-video.title: Watch the Video
M06-2-video.studentContent:
🎬
Play Practice Video
▶ Video A▶ Video B
First watch: feel the overall impression. Second watch: watch and fill out the form
M06-2-video.presenterContent:
💡 Video Selection Tips:
Choose moderately complex company videos with identifiable features but not too difficult. Recommend Tier A Skeletons (e.g., HPVC or HBVC) so participants can quickly identify the structure.
M06-2-video.notes: Play the video twice (~3 minutes), then participants independently fill out analysis forms for 5 minutes. Instructor and assistants walk around observing but don't intervene.

M06-2-writing.title: Independent Analysis
M06-2-writing.studentContent:
📝
Fill Out the Analysis Form Independently
Analyze the video you just watched using the BMS Complete Analysis Form
M06-2-writing.presenterContent:
▶ Instructor Actions:
  • Walk around observing participants' analysis directions
  • Don't intervene, but note typical situations
  • Give a 1-minute warning: "One minute left"
M06-2-writing.notes: 5-minute independent analysis. Instructor and assistants walk around, observe analysis directions, don't intervene but note typical situations.

M06-3-title.title: Part 3
M06-3-title.subtitle: Group Discussion
M06-3-title.notes: Participants discuss analysis results in groups. Focus on whether they're using framework language.

M06-3-tasks.title: Discussion Tasks
M06-3-tasks.studentContent:
🤝 Group Discussion

1️⃣ Align Analysis Results

What Skeleton did you identify? What Strategy did you choose? See if everyone got the same answers.

2️⃣ Discuss Differences

If your judgments differ, discuss why.

3️⃣ Identify Issues

What are 1-2 areas where this video could be improved?

⚠️ Use Framework Language!
❌ "I think something's off here"✅ "The HOOK Strategy should be PAIN, not SUSPENSE"
❌ "The opening isn't engaging enough"✅ "The HOOK Strategy could switch to CONTRAST to enhance the contrast effect"
M06-3-tasks.presenterContent:
💡 Observation Guide:
  • Focus on whether participants are using framework language
  • Hearing "something feels off" → Guide them to say "Soul Profile's target_audience doesn't match the actual content"
  • Hearing "the pace is a bit slow" → Guide them to say "Meat Profile's pacing could be adjusted to medium"
M06-3-tasks.notes: Groups of 4-5 discuss. Instructor and assistants walk around, guiding participants to use terminology when they hear 'feeling words.'

M06-4-title.title: Part 4
M06-4-title.subtitle: Analysis Review
M06-4-title.notes: Ask 2-3 groups to share analysis results; instructor provides review demonstration.

M06-4-sharing.title: Group Sharing
M06-4-sharing.studentContent:
🤝 Group Sharing
Each group has 1-2 minutes to share core findings:
  1. What is this video's Skeleton?
  2. What Strategy does each Section use?
  3. What issues did you find?
M06-4-sharing.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
OK, time's up. Let's hear some groups' analysis results.

Who wants to go first? Use 1-2 minutes to share your core findings: What's the video's Skeleton? What Strategy does each Section use? What issues did you find?
M06-4-sharing.notes: Ask 2-3 groups to quickly share their analysis results.

M06-4-demo.title: Instructor Review Demonstration
M06-4-demo.studentContent:
💡 Review Demo: "Feeling Words" → "Framework Language"

Review DimensionDemo Script
Acknowledge Strengths"You identified that the HOOK uses SUSPENSE strategy — very accurate!"
Point Out Gaps"Meat Profile and Soul don't quite match — Soul targets young women, but pacing is set to low"
Transform Vocabulary"The pace is a bit slow" → "Meat Profile's pacing could be adjusted to medium"
This is the thinking mode you'll be practicing next — transforming "feeling words" into "framework language."
M06-4-demo.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Great work. Let me add some review comments.

You identified that the HOOK section uses SUSPENSE strategy — very accurate. SUSPENSE belongs to the HAND group, and its core psychological mechanism is creating information gaps that make viewers "want to know the answer."

But there's something worth noting — Meat Profile and Soul don't quite match. The Soul layer defines the target audience as young women, but the video's pacing is set to low, which means a slower pace. Young audiences usually need a faster rhythm.

Notice what I just did — I demonstrated transforming "feeling words" into "framework language." This is the thinking mode you'll be practicing next.
M06-4-demo.notes: Key demonstration: how to transform 'feeling words' into 'framework language.' Acknowledge strengths first, then point out gaps.

M06-5-title.title: Part 5
M06-5-title.subtitle: Hands-On Design
M06-5-title.notes: Each group receives a product info packet and completes Skeleton + Strategy design.

M06-5-task.title: Design Task
M06-5-task.studentContent:
🤝 Group Hands-On
Each group gets a product. Complete the following in 15 minutes:

1️⃣ Choose a Skeleton Type

What skeleton fits your product? HPVC? HBVC? HPRVC? Or another one?

2️⃣ Design Strategy for Each Section

What's the L0 group? Which L1 strategy? How does L2 execute specifically?
Remember, every choice needs a reason!

💡 Use the quick reference guide to help with decisions. Focus on discussing "why" you chose it, not just "what" you chose.
M06-5-task.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Great, you've finished analyzing others' videos. Now it's your turn to design!

Each group will get a product. Complete the following in 15 minutes: First, choose a Skeleton type; Second, design Strategy for each Section (L0/L1/L2). Remember, every choice needs a reason!
💡 Observation Tips:
  • Watch whether groups are discussing "why" instead of just "what"
  • Guide with questions: "Does your product need to build trust first? Or can you show results directly?"
  • Don't give answers directly — let participants reason using framework language
M06-5-task.notes: Distribute product info packets to each group. Instructor walks around using questions to guide thinking — don't give direct answers.

M06-5-scoring.title: Scoring Reference
M06-5-scoring.studentContent:
DimensionWeightScoring Criteria
🦴 Bone Design30 ptsReasonable Skeleton selection + appropriate Strategy pairing for each Section
👻 Soul Design25 ptsComplete and specific SoulProfile with clear target audience
🥩 Meat Design20 ptsMeatProfile matches Bone/Soul
💡 Design Logic25 ptsCan explain the reasoning behind each choice using framework language
M06-5-scoring.presenterContent: 💡 Scoring Tips: The scoring reference is for instructor reference only — no need to score item by item in class. The key is whether participants can clearly explain "why."
M06-5-scoring.notes: Scoring criteria is for reference only. The core is whether participants can explain their choices using framework language.

M06-6-title.title: Part 6
M06-6-title.subtitle: Quick Presentations
M06-6-title.notes: Each group sends a representative to present, 2 minutes per group.

M06-6-present.title: Presentation Points
M06-6-present.studentContent:
Each group has 2 minutes to present. Focus on three things:

1️⃣ What Skeleton did you choose? Why?

2️⃣ What Strategy for each Section? Why this combination?

3️⃣ How did you define the Soul layer? How does it work with the Bone choices?

💡 Other groups should listen carefully and ask questions using framework language.
For example: "Why didn't you choose CONTRAST for the HOOK section?" "If the Soul layer's audience changes, should the Strategy change too?"
M06-6-present.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
OK, time's up! Each group, send a representative to present your design in 2 minutes.

Focus on three things: What Skeleton did you choose? Why? What Strategy for each Section? Why this combination? How did you define the Soul layer? How does it work with the Bone choices?

Other groups, listen carefully and feel free to ask questions using framework language.
💡 Review Tips:
  • Acknowledge strengths first, then point out areas for optimization
  • Use framework language reviews as demonstration
  • Encourage other groups to ask questions using framework language
M06-6-present.notes: 2 minutes per group presentation. Instructor reviews each group, encourages other groups to ask questions using framework language.

M06-6-diagnosis.title: Common Issue Diagnosis
M06-6-diagnosis.studentContent:
SymptomDiagnosis Direction
HOOK not engaging enoughCheck HOOK section's Strategy L1 — recommend SUSPENSE, CONTRAST, PAIN, etc.
Video logic doesn't flowCheck Skeleton type — is the Section arrangement logical? Are transitions natural?
Content doesn't match target usersCheck SoulProfile — is the target audience clear? Do language style and emotion arc match?
CTA lacks powerCheck CTA section Strategy — recommend URGENCY (URGENCY_TIME / URGENCY_STOCK) to create urgency
Visuals disconnected from contentCheck MeatProfile alignment with Bone/Soul — do visual style and pacing serve the overall strategy?
M06-6-diagnosis.presenterContent: 💡 Teaching Tip: Reference this diagnosis table when reviewing each group's design. Use framework terminology to give specific improvement suggestions.
M06-6-diagnosis.notes: Common issue diagnosis table. Helps instructors and participants quickly locate problems.

M06-7-title.title: Part 7
M06-7-title.subtitle: Wrap-Up
M06-7-title.notes: Review BMS framework, emphasize framework value.

M06-7-summary.title: BMS Framework Summary
M06-7-summary.studentContent:
Bone

骨

Defines Structure

Section building blocks
Skeleton structural framework
Strategy persuasion techniques

Soul

魂

Explains Why It Works

Target Audience, Trigger Scenario
Reward Type, Conflict Type, Emotion Arc

Meat

肉

Defines How It's Presented

Visual Style, Voice Design
Pacing Control, Visual Elements

You've learned a way of thinking —
Analyze content, design content, and discuss content using framework language
M06-7-summary.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Over these two days of training, we've covered the entire Bone-Meat-Soul framework.

The BMS framework — Bone defines structure, including Section, Skeleton, and Strategy at three levels; Soul explains why it works, defining target audience and emotion arc; Meat defines how it's presented, specifying visual style and production standards. All three layers work together — none can be missing.

But more importantly, you've learned a way of thinking — analyze content, design content, and discuss content using framework language.
M06-7-summary.notes: Framework summary. Emphasize that the shift in thinking is more important than memorizing terminology.

M06-7-before-after.title: Before vs After Training
M06-7-before-after.studentContent:
❌ Before Training
"This video is pretty good"
"That one doesn't work"
✅ After Training
"This video uses an HPVC skeleton,
the HOOK section uses SUSPENSE strategy,
but the CTA section lacks URGENCY
to drive action"
That's progress!
M06-7-before-after.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Before the training, when you saw a video you might have said "this is pretty good" or "that doesn't work." Now? You can say "this video uses an HPVC skeleton, the HOOK section uses SUSPENSE strategy, but the CTA section lacks URGENCY to drive action."

That's progress!
M06-7-before-after.notes: Use Before/After comparison to let participants visually feel their progress.

M06-7-action.title: Three Things to Do When You Get Back
M06-7-action.studentContent:
1️⃣ Analyze Existing Videos

Do a complete BMS analysis of your company's existing videos using the analysis form you learned today

2️⃣ Framework-First Design

Next time you create a new video, design with the framework first, then write the script

3️⃣ Unify Team Language

When your team discusses content, try using framework language — you'll find it's much more efficient
M06-7-action.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
When you get back, I recommend doing three things:

First, do a complete BMS analysis of your company's existing videos using the analysis form you learned today;

Second, next time you create a new video, design with the framework first, then write the script;

Third, when your team discusses content, try using framework language — you'll find it's much more efficient.
M06-7-action.notes: Three action recommendations to help participants continue applying after the training.

M06-8-qa.title: Common Questions
M06-8-qa.studentContent:
❓ Q&A Session
Participant QuestionSuggested Response
What if my analysis differs from others?That's normal! That's exactly the value of discussion. The key is discussing with framework language, not "I think"
What if there's not enough time?Done is better than perfect. Complete the most important parts first, and continue with details after class
I'm not sure if my choice is correct?The framework doesn't have one right answer — the key is being able to clearly explain "why" you made that choice
How do I use this when I get back?Start by analyzing existing videos — first break them down with the framework, then try designing new content using the framework
M06-8-qa.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Let's save the last 10 minutes for questions. About the BMS framework, about practical application — any question is welcome.
M06-8-qa.notes: Open Q&A. Refer to this table for common questions.

M06-end.title: Training Complete ✓
M06-end.subtitle: Thank you for your participation and dedication over two days!
M06-end.description: Create better content with the BMS Framework
M06-end.notes: Group photo, training ends. Inform about follow-up support channels and resources.
